text:
"The net cast at noon catches only shadows."

This means that timing and patience matter more than effort alone. If you act too eagerly, too visibly, too obviously, you will find nothing of substance. The fish are not where everyone can see them.

The proverb imagines a fisherman who, frustrated with waiting, casts his net when the sun is highest and the water most disturbed. He works hard. He catches nothing real.

The lesson: sometimes the right moment is quiet, less obvious. Act when conditions favor results, not when your impatience demands it. Effort without timing is just noise dressed up as work.
